title: Season of Annunciation

infobox.value: [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), [Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malabar Catholic Church"), [Syriac Orthodox Church](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church"), [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church"), [Syro-Malankara Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malankara Catholic Church"), [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church")

or **Season of Announcements** ([Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"): ܫܒܘܥܐ ܕܣܒܪܐ),[[1]](#cite_note-:3-1) also known by various Syriac [transliterations](/wiki/Transliterations "Transliterations") such as **Subara**, **Suvara**, **Subbara**, or **Suboro** ([Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"): ܣܘܒܪܐ),[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [liturgical season](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"). The name of the season is in reference to the [Annunciation to the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOzer20193-3) the announcement by the [Archangel Gabriel](/wiki/Archangel_Gabriel "Archangel Gabriel") to the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"), that she would [conceive and bear](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation") a son through a [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") and become the mother of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Syriac Christian liturgical season beginning in December

This article is about the Syriac Christian liturgical season in December. For the feast day in March, see [Feast of the Annunciation](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation"). For the Biblical event, see [Annunciation](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation").

"Subara" redirects here. For the Serbian hat, see [Šubara](/wiki/%C5%A0ubara "Šubara").

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Liturgical_year "Template:Liturgical year") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Liturgical_year "Template talk:Liturgical year") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical_year "Special:EditPage/Template:Liturgical year") |

The **Season of Annunciation** or **Season of Announcements** ([Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"): ܫܒܘܥܐ ܕܣܒܪܐ),[[1]](#cite_note-:3-1) also known by various Syriac [transliterations](/wiki/Transliterations "Transliterations") such as **Subara**, **Suvara**, **Subbara**, or **Suboro** ([Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"): ܣܘܒܪܐ),[[2]](#cite_note-2) is a [liturgical season](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") in [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"). The name of the season is in reference to the [Annunciation to the Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Annunciation "Annunciation"),[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOzer20193-3) the announcement by the [Archangel Gabriel](/wiki/Archangel_Gabriel "Archangel Gabriel") to the [Blessed Virgin Mary](/wiki/Mary,_mother_of_Jesus "Mary, mother of Jesus"), that she would [conceive and bear](/wiki/Fertilisation "Fertilisation") a son through a [virgin birth](/wiki/Virgin_birth_of_Jesus "Virgin birth of Jesus") and become the mother of [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ").

The Period of Annunciation is present in both the [East](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite") and [West Syriac](/wiki/West_Syriac_Rite "West Syriac Rite") rites, covering the Sundays before [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) In the [East Syriac tradition](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite"), the liturgical year starts with the Period of Annunciation, and is equivalent to the season of [Advent](/wiki/Advent "Advent") in the [Western liturgical tradition](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites"). Like Advent, it is a time for the celebration of the [Nativity of Jesus](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus"). But unlike Advent, the whole season is a celebration of the Nativity, while in Advent the first 24 days are a preparation for the Nativity on 25 December. Traditionally, in both rites, the depiction of the Annunciation has been popular in [Syriac literature](/wiki/Syriac_literature "Syriac literature") and art.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Historically, the Annunciation season in the [Syro-Malabar Church](/wiki/Syro-Malabar_Church "Syro-Malabar Church") came to an end in early January with the feast of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_season "Epiphany season") (*[Pindikuthi Perunnal](/wiki/Pindikuthi_Perunnal "Pindikuthi Perunnal")*) which was the more celebrated feast. But by the 20th century, due to influence of the Western tradition and the secular world, Christmas day (December 25) became the focal point of the season. The season is also celebrated on March 25 in the [Feast of the Annunciation](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation").[[6]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMon20229-6)

## East Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_Annunciation&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: East Syriac Rite")]

In the [East Syriac Rite](/wiki/East_Syriac_Rite "East Syriac Rite"), the Season of Annunciation, like Advent in the [Roman Rite](/wiki/Roman_Rite "Roman Rite"), marks the start of the liturgical year,[[1]](#cite_note-:3-1) and begins on the Sunday that falls between November 27 and December 3 inclusive.[[7]](#cite_note-moolan13-7) The season extends past Advent to also include [Christmastide](/wiki/Christmastide "Christmastide").[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadhavathu20166-9) However, the present-day [liturgical calendar of the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar_of_the_Syro-Malabar_Catholic_Church "Liturgical calendar of the Syro-Malabar Catholic Church") (one of the churches that uses the East Syriac Rite) regards "Annunciation" and "Nativity" as separate seasons, with the Season of Annunciation ending prior to Christmas.[[10]](#cite_note-10) This is believed to be due to [monastic](/wiki/Monasticism "Monasticism") influences,[[1]](#cite_note-:3-1) and the practice is known as *Irupathanchu Nombu*;[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadhavathu201676-11) in 1971, the liturgy for Subara was revised in [Malayalam](/wiki/Malayalam "Malayalam") by the Syro-Malabar Central Liturgical Committee.[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMadhavathu201622-12) The church considers the [Feast of the Annunciation](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Annunciation "Feast of the Annunciation") as a liturgical holiday.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

In the [Assyrian Church of the East](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East"), on the second Sunday of the season, the congregation will hear Gabriel's Annunciation to the Virgin Mary, followed by gospels the two Sundays after telling the mission of [John the Baptist](/wiki/John_the_Baptist "John the Baptist") and the proclamation to [Saint Joseph](/wiki/Saint_Joseph "Saint Joseph").[[1]](#cite_note-:3-1) The season closes with the Feast of the Annunciation, and lasts about seven weeks.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The church has its own unique hymn assigned to the Book of Ḥudra for both the Season of the Annunciation and the Nativity;[[15]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESada202163-15)[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESada202166-68-16) the opening of the Ḥudra begins with the first four Sundays of Subbara.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

A separate commemoration of Subbara was added in the rite of the [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church").[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) For the church, the season is composed of four weeks and usually begins on a Sunday (either December 1 or the first Sunday before).[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## West Syriac Rite

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_Annunciation&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: West Syriac Rite")]

Churches that follow the [West Syriac Rite](/wiki/West_Syriac_Rite "West Syriac Rite") call this period the Season of Annunciation (as with the [Syro-Malankara Catholic Church](/wiki/Syro-Malankara_Catholic_Church "Syro-Malankara Catholic Church"))[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMon20225-20) or the Season of Announcements (as with the [Maronite Church](/wiki/Maronite_Church "Maronite Church")).[[21]](#cite_note-21) In this rite, the season begins six Sundays before Christmas,[[7]](#cite_note-moolan13-7) making it coterminous with Advent in the [Ambrosian Rite](/wiki/Ambrosian_Rite "Ambrosian Rite").

In the [Syriac Orthodox](/wiki/Syriac_Orthodox_Church "Syriac Orthodox Church") and [Malankara Orthodox Syrian Churches](/wiki/Malankara_Orthodox_Syrian_Church "Malankara Orthodox Syrian Church"), the Suboro is considered one of the most important feasts in the liturgy; the [Holy Qurobo](/wiki/Holy_Qurobo "Holy Qurobo") is required for recital even if the Feast of the Annunciation falls on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), where it would otherwise be prohibited.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23)

### Assyrian tradition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Season_of_Annunciation&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Assyrian tradition")]

[Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrians "Assyrians") in the SOC (namely from [Tur Abdin](/wiki/Tur_Abdin "Tur Abdin")) maintain unique traditions in honor of the season of Suboro. This tradition, also called Suboro, takes place annually on March 25.[[4]](#cite_note-:0-4) A [cultural](/wiki/Assyrian_culture "Assyrian culture")/[culinary](/wiki/Assyrian_cuisine "Assyrian cuisine") tradition involves kneading unleavened dough and sprinkling grains on top; the daughter of the family then puts white and red yarn in the shape of the [Cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") on the dough[[24]](#cite_note-24) before walking around town.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOzer20193-3) During the making of the dough, families will also sing a [hymn](/wiki/Syriac_sacral_music "Syriac sacral music"), *The Bread of Life is Mine*.[[25]](#cite_note-:1-25) All the available grains in the house will be added to the dough when cooking.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-26) The dough is then taken to the roof of the house and left to sit while hymns are sung; after church service, the bread is placed under the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") and then served to the congregation.

The white and red yarn are also used to create [wristbands](/wiki/Wristband "Wristband"), representing the interwined nature of the humanity (red) and [divine resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") (white) of Christ.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-26) The wristbands by themselves represent the appearance of the Archangel Gabriel to the Virgin Mary.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Some bracelets also include black yarn, which can represent either the [death of Christ](/wiki/Death_of_Christ "Death of Christ") or combine with the other colors to represent the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity").[[25]](#cite_note-:1-25)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The wristbands are worn until [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter"), but they may also be worn until the Christmas holiday. Sometimes, the wristbands are burned under a pot of [rice pudding](/wiki/Rice_pudding "Rice pudding").[[26]](#cite_note-:4-26)

Suboro is additionally practiced by [Yazidis](/wiki/Yazidis "Yazidis") and the [Mhallami](/wiki/Mhallami "Mhallami"), an Arabic-speaking ethnic group theorized to have Assyrian origins; the Mhallami create blue and white bracelets called Basımbar.[[26]](#cite_note-:4-26) The tradition is not as prominent among Mhallami compared to Assyrians, but is celebrated by a few today.[[29]](#cite_note-29) Yazidis make bracelets on a special day during the year with additional green and yellow colors, and hang it on several objects like cars and houses, as well as their wrists.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Another tradition from the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"), known as [Martenitsa](/wiki/Martenitsa "Martenitsa"), is similar to Suboro.
